name: Jothand
description: Machine-readable token definitions for the Jothand hybrid shorthand system. Suitable for use as LLM context or as the foundation for parser and renderer tooling.

soundSymbols.description: Unvoiced t. Uncrossed t stroke in handwriting. Represented as ⏐ (VERTICAL LINE EXTENSION) in digital text to avoid conflicts with Markdown pipe characters.
Voiced or unvoiced th. Full crossed t in handwriting.
Arched stroke sitting on the baseline.
Wide inverted arch. Visually much wider than a normal u-shape.
Unvoiced s or hard c, as in 'see' or 'cent'.
Voiced s (z sound), as in 'has' or 'buzz'. Also used to form plurals: appending ˙ to a phonetic word makes it plural (e.g. ⏐rt˙ = truths).
Hard k or hard c sound, as in 'cat'. Note: q retains its standard English value (k+w) and should not be written as c◡.
Wide underscore stroke. Must be visually distinct from a connector stroke between letters.
ing ending. Used wherever the pronunciation is 'ing'.
nd or nt consonant blend, as in 'and' or 'evident'. Also doubles as the word abbreviation for 'and'. Context determines which use applies.
at vowel+consonant sound. Also doubles as the word abbreviation for 'at', 'that', or 'they'. Context determines which use applies.

wordAbbreviations.note: Context determines meaning. Overlaps with the at-sound symbol.
Represents any general or unknown quantity. Context determines the specific meaning.
Overlaps with the nd/nt sound blend.
Two-character form; cannot participate in chains.

chainingRules.description: A chain is a sequence of whole-word tokens written without spaces. Every token in a chain must be exactly one character. Multi-character phonetic abbreviations (e.g. 'lc' for 'like', 'hv' for 'have') cannot participate in chains and must be space-separated from adjacent tokens.

examples.gloss: to be or not to be

interpretations: at-sound within a phonetic word (sound symbol)
nd/nt consonant blend within a phonetic word (sound symbol)

principles.vowelOmission: Vowels are generally omitted and recovered from context.
principles.boundaryVowels: Leading and trailing vowel sounds on a word are often written out explicitly for clarity (e.g. 'only' rather than '_l', 'i⏐.lf' rather than '⏐.lf' for 'itself').
principles.preferOriginal: When a shorthand form is no shorter than the original letter, prefer the original letter. For example, q represents (k+w) in standard English and should remain q rather than be written as c◡.
principles.pluralization: Plurals are formed by appending ˙ (voiced s) to the phonetic word.
principles.punctuation: Commas, periods, parentheses, and hyphens overlap with Jothand sound symbols and must not be used as prose punctuation. Exclamation marks (!) and question marks (?) are safe and do not conflict with any Jothand form.
principles.ambiguity: Jothand tolerates more ambiguity than formal systems. The writer chooses compression level based on how recoverable the result will be from context. There is no rule that forces abbreviation.
